result:
The old man in the apartment above mine taps his foot in the same spot at three in the morning. It’s a soft, rhythmic thump—like a heartbeat muffled by floorboards and dreams. I used to curse it, pressing my pillow against my ears, convinced it was the sound of insomnia masquerading as a secret. But last Tuesday, I saw him shuffling down the hallway, his face a map of wrinkles and worn kindness, and he nodded at me. I nodded back.

Now I listen differently. I imagine he’s dancing with a ghost, or rocking a phantom child, or counting the seconds until the sun dares to return. His foot is a metronome for a world that won’t keep still. And I realize: we are all tapping to something. The barista’s fidgeting with her ring, the bus driver’s hum, the dog that scratches at the door. These tiny, repetitive motions are anchors in the chaos—proof that we are still here, still insisting on being present, even in the hollow hours.

So tonight, when the thumping begins, I won’t fight it. I’ll let it be the bass line to my own quiet thoughts. And perhaps, in some strange way, we are dancing together—two strangers, connected by wood and silence, keeping time.
